era_context: Late Bronze Age collapse at its peak. Egypt under Ramesses III is managing the aftermath of major Sea Peoples invasions and maintaining state institutions despite external pressure. The Hittite Empire is in its final year or has just collapsed (c. 1180 BCE, with dating uncertainty of ±5 years). Mycenaean Greece is fully post-palatial, with populations dispersing and settlement patterns radically altered. This is the nadir of Bronze Age civilisation in the Eastern Mediterranean.

events.title: Ramesses III's Year 10: consolidation and temple building
events.description: In the tenth year of his reign, Ramesses III continues to consolidate Egyptian state power and invest in temple construction and religious institutions. The Medinet Habu mortuary temple complex is under active construction and decoration. This period shows that despite external military pressures, the Egyptian state retains sufficient resources and organisational capacity to undertake major building projects. The investment in temples and religious institutions suggests a strategy of legitimising royal authority through religious and cultural means.

sources.name: Temple inscriptions and reliefs at Medinet Habu

events.certainty_note: Dating to Year 10 of Ramesses III is based on regnal year counts in Egyptian sources. The broader context of temple construction is confirmed by archaeological evidence.
cross_references: Part of Ramesses III's reign and temple-building programme
Reflects Egyptian state capacity despite external pressures

events.title: Hittite Empire collapse (or final years)
events.description: The Hittite Empire either collapses in this year or has just collapsed (dating is uncertain ±3–5 years). The last cuneiform texts from Hattusa date to approximately this period. The empire's administrative and military apparatus ceases to function; the capital Hattusa is abandoned or destroyed; and Anatolia fragments into smaller successor states and kingdoms. The causes remain debated—invasion by external enemies, internal civil conflict, environmental stress (drought), or systemic collapse—but the result is the end of one of the Bronze Age's great imperial powers.
key_figures: Suppiluliuma II (last known Hittite Great King)

sources.name: Hittite cuneiform texts from Hattusa (latest datable examples)
Archaeological stratigraphy from Hattusa and other Hittite sites

events.certainty_note: The Hittite collapse is dated to c. 1180 BCE based on the latest cuneiform texts, but absolute dating is uncertain (±5–10 years). The precise year of collapse cannot be determined from available evidence.
cross_references: Part of broader Late Bronze Age collapse
Contemporary with Ramesses III's reign and Sea Peoples disruptions

events.title: Post-palatial settlement patterns in Greece
events.description: By 1182 BCE, the Mycenaean palace civilisation has been fully replaced by post-palatial settlement patterns. Populations have dispersed from major palace centres; settlement sizes are smaller; material culture is simplified; and writing (Linear B) has disappeared. Some populations are migrating to new regions (Ionia, Cyprus, the Levantine coast). The archaeological record shows clear discontinuity with the Bronze Age world. This represents a fundamental reorganisation of Greek society and the beginning of the Early Iron Age in the Aegean.

sources.name: Archaeological settlement surveys and pottery sequences from Greece
Absence of Linear B texts after c. 1200–1180 BCE

events.certainty_note: Archaeological evidence for post-palatial settlement patterns is clear and well-documented. The transition from palatial to post-palatial society is secure; absolute dating of the transition is debated (±10–20 years).
cross_references: Related to broader Late Bronze Age collapse

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Hittite collapse to c. 1180 BCE is based on the latest cuneiform texts, but some scholars argue for earlier or later dates (±10 years). The causes of Hittite collapse remain genuinely uncertain; the 'invasion hypothesis' (attributed to Sea Peoples or other external enemies) is one theory among several, and some scholars emphasise internal conflict or environmental stress instead. The notion that the Mycenaean collapse and Hittite collapse are causally related is speculative; they may have been independent phenomena. The 'Late Bronze Age collapse' as a unified, synchronous event has been questioned; some scholars prefer to describe regional collapses with different causes and timings.
historiographic_note: 1182 BCE is a year where the historical record is fragmentary and uncertain. Egyptian sources are relatively abundant, but Hittite sources are ending, and Greek sources are absent. This creates a perspective skewed toward Egypt. The causes of the Late Bronze Age collapse remain one of the most debated topics in ancient history, with scholars proposing invasion, internal conflict, environmental stress, systems collapse, or combinations thereof. The 'Sea Peoples' remain poorly understood; they are known primarily through Egyptian and Hittite descriptions, and their own origins and motivations are uncertain. Modern scholarship increasingly uses environmental evidence (ice cores, tree rings, pollen, isotope analysis) to reconstruct climate and drought patterns, but correlating these with specific historical events remains speculative.

graph_edges.note: Hittite collapse is contemporary with Ramesses III's Sea Peoples campaigns
Post-palatial settlement patterns lead to Greek Dark Ages
